metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a grep-based oracle to AC3 for each named ADR section (e.g. `grep -Eq 'resource-server-only' docs/adr/NNN-mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'CVE\|GHSA' docs/adr/NNN-mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'deferred' docs/adr/NNN-mcp-auth.md`) so completeness is mechanically verifiable.
evidence: AC3 ends after listing required content with no 'observable oracle' clause, unlike AC1 which includes an explicit `test -f … && grep -Eq …` command
The other content-bearing AC (AC1) demonstrates the pattern of anchoring each required section to a grep — AC3 omits this entirely
findings.finding: AC3 (ADR) has no observable oracle / proving command — only a prose checklist of required content. An executor cannot verify that all named decisions (composite audience choke point, CVE citations, deferred items) are actually present in the written ADR without a grep or script command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'An ADR file exists under docs/adr/ recording…'
scenarios: An agent writes an ADR that omits the CVE citations or the composite-audience motivation; AC3 passes because the file exists but the plan's content requirements are unverified
A reviewer cannot run a single command to confirm done-ness for this criterion
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: observable oracle: `ADR=$(ls docs/adr/*mcp-auth*.md | head -1) && grep -Eq 'resource-server-only' "$ADR" && grep -Eq 'composite.{0,20}audience|audience.{0,20}choke' "$ADR" && grep -Eq 'CVE|GHSA' "$ADR" && grep -Eq 'deferred' "$ADR"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before authoring the ADR, verify each cited advisory identifier (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) against NVD/MITRE/GitHub Security Advisories/OSV; replace any phantom or future-dated ID with the correct verifiable identifier, a public disclosure URL, or — if no published advisory exists — a prose description of the threat class with a reference to the relevant RFC/standard instead.
evidence: Plan text: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
Plan text: 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
CVE-2026-* are future-dated; no NVD entries can be verified; PVE-* is not a recognized advisory namespace
Repo-wide search for all four identifiers returns zero results in source, docs, or existing ADRs — only in plan-review cache artifacts
Compare: existing ADR 0046 cites verifiable prior art (gittuf NDSS 2025 paper, TUF spec v1.0.31, RFC 8725, NIST SP 800-218, CVE-2026-44544 for gittuf) with traceable references
findings.finding: The ADR (as specified in the plan) is required to cite four specific security identifiers — GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, and PVE-2026-93393 — as the motivating prior art for transport-hardening and composite-audience decisions, but none are verifiable: CVE-2026-* are future-dated identifiers with no NVD/MITRE records, PVE-2026-93393 does not match any recognized advisory namespace (NVD, GitHub Security Advisories, OSV, or PyPA), and repo search finds these strings only in prior review cache files — never in source or existing ADRs. An ADR meant for 'rigorous security review' that grounds architectural decisions in phantom identifiers fails the auditability purpose it declares.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)' and 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
scenarios: A security auditor attempts to look up CVE-2026-40575 or PVE-2026-93393 and finds no record — the ADR's transport-hardening and composite-audience rationale becomes unverifiable, undermining the 'rigorous security review' the plan promises.
Future maintainers cite this ADR in a compliance review; phantom CVEs are flagged as fabricated, casting doubt on all ADR claims.
The composite-audience design's sole stated motivator (PVE-2026-93393) is phantom — the design has no verifiable prior-art grounding in the ADR.
findings.suggested_fix: For each identifier: (1) look up on nvd.nist.gov and github.com/advisories; (2) if found, use the canonical ID and link; (3) if the advisory doesn't exist yet, describe the threat class (e.g. 'DNS rebinding as described in RFC 6454 §7 and the WHATWG Fetch origin model') without claiming a specific CVE; (4) replace PVE-2026-93393 with either a real OSV/PyPA advisory URL or a prose statement of the audience-confusion threat class with a reference to RFC 8707 §2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unanchored `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` oracle in AC1 with a heading-anchored grep that matches the actual residual-risks section heading (e.g., `grep -Eq '^## .*[Rr]esidual [Rr]isk' docs/mcp-auth.md`) to prevent a trivial substring insertion from satisfying the criterion.
evidence: Plan AC1 oracle: `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
The What section labels the same content 'Residual risks (documented, not fixed here)' — not 'out of scope' — so the oracle string is not derived from the described section name.
All other proving commands in AC1 use anchored heading patterns (e.g., `grep -Eq '^## 1\. Enabling'`, `grep -Eq '^### \`static\`'`), but the residual-risks oracle uses an unanchored substring match.
This is an instruction-locality antipattern: the oracle encodes a specific string that the document must contain, incentivizing a minimal literal insertion rather than substantive section authorship.
findings.finding: The AC1 grep oracle requires `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` to prove the residual-risks section exists, but this is a pink-elephant/instruction-locality pattern: an executor can satisfy the oracle by placing the literal string 'out of scope' anywhere in the file (e.g., a footnote or unrelated paragraph) without actually authoring the required residual-risks content. All other section oracles in AC1 anchor to specific heading syntax ('^## ...', '^### `...`'), but 'out of scope' is a free-floating substring match with no heading anchor, making it the weakest oracle in the set.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — observable oracle grep command: `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
scenarios: An executor adds a passing mention of 'out of scope' in a config note or unrelated paragraph, causing AC1's oracle to exit 0 while the residual-risks section (authenticated≠authorized, per-tool authz deferral, rate-limiting delegation, static-token entropy) is entirely absent.
A reviewer checking the oracle output cannot distinguish a compliant residual-risks section from an accidental string occurrence.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` with `grep -Eq '^## .*[Rr]esidual [Rr]isk' docs/mcp-auth.md` (or whichever heading the plan intends, e.g., `'^## Residual risks'`), consistent with the anchored style used for all other section oracles in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the loose `grep -Eq 'out of scope'` oracle clause in AC1 with anchored greps for the specific residual-risk headings/terms, e.g. `grep -Eq 'Residual [Rr]isk' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'authenticated.*authorized|authorized.*authenticated' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'rate.limit' docs/mcp-auth.md`.
evidence: The oracle checks for the literal string 'out of scope' with no word-boundary or section anchor

The 'What' section lists specific residual-risk topics: 'authenticated≠authorized', 'per-tool authz deferral', 'rate-limiting delegation', 'static-token entropy' — none of these distinct terms appear in the oracle
findings.finding: The AC1 grep oracle uses `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` to verify the residual-risks section, but 'out of scope' is a common phrase that can appear in unrelated contexts (e.g. the proxy or auth-strategy sections), creating a substring false-positive (VERIFY-COMMAND defect type 1) that would pass even if the residual-risks content is absent.
findings.location: AC1 proving command — `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
scenarios: The author writes a sentence like 'DCR is out of scope' in the proxy section; the oracle passes but the dedicated Residual Risks section with authenticated≠authorized content is missing
The rate-limiting and static-token-entropy requirements from 'What' have no grep anchor at all
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md` with: `grep -Eq '^## .*[Rr]esidual' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'authenticated.{1,10}authorized' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'rate.lim' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq '128.bit\|static.token.entropy' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 grep oracle to include a check for the one-instance-per-type flat-schema limitation, e.g. `grep -Eq 'one instance per' docs/mcp-auth.md` (or equivalent phrase), so the oracle fully verifies the stated requirement.
evidence: What: 'the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary. State the flat-schema limitation: one instance per strategy type'
AC1 body text: 'the `auth_strategies` vocabulary + the one-instance-per-type limitation'
AC1 oracle: no grep for 'one-instance-per-type', 'one instance per', 'flat-schema', or equivalent phrase
findings.finding: AC1's grep oracle does not verify the 'one-instance-per-strategy-type' limitation even though both the What section and the AC1 text explicitly require it to be documented, creating a gap between the stated requirement and its observable verification.
findings.location: AC1 (grep oracle vs. AC1 body text and What section)
scenarios: An implementer writes mcp-auth.md with all required headings but omits the one-instance-per-type limitation; all greps pass and the AC is considered done, but a key operator-facing constraint is undocumented.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC1 shell oracle: `&& grep -Eq 'one instance per' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add grep clauses to AC1's oracle for the nginx/ALB example and `auth_resource_server_url`/`WWW-Authenticate` content, e.g. `grep -Eq 'nginx|ALB' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'auth_resource_server_url' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'WWW-Authenticate' docs/mcp-auth.md`.
evidence: 'What' states: 'Include a concrete nginx/ALB header + allowlist example' — no grep in AC1 checks for nginx or ALB

'What' states: '`auth_resource_server_url` MUST be the external https URL (it drives the served PRM `resource` and the `WWW-Authenticate: resource_metadata` URL)' — no grep in AC1 checks for WWW-Authenticate or resource_metadata
AC1 grep only checks: `grep -Eq 'Behind a proxy' docs/mcp-auth.md` for the entire proxy section
findings.finding: The AC1 oracle does not verify two content requirements explicitly listed in the 'What' section: (a) a concrete nginx/ALB header + allowlist example, and (b) that `auth_resource_server_url` drives the served PRM `resource` and `WWW-Authenticate` URL. These are substantive operator-safety requirements with no grep anchor.
findings.location: AC1 proving command vs. 'What' section bullets for behind-a-proxy deployment
scenarios: The author writes a 'Behind a proxy' heading with one sentence but no nginx/ALB example; the AC passes
The critical operator warning about auth_resource_server_url driving WWW-Authenticate is omitted; the AC passes
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC1 oracle: `&& grep -Eq 'nginx|ALB' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'auth_resource_server_url' docs/mcp-auth.md && grep -Eq 'WWW-Authenticate' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the AC1 oracle with the plan's own vocabulary: replace `grep -Eq 'out of scope'` with a grep that matches the actual intended phrase (e.g. `grep -Eq 'deferred|out of scope'`) or add 'out of scope' to the What section so authors know to use it.
evidence: AC1 oracle: `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`
Plan What section: 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred'
ADR section: 'the explicitly deferred items (AS/DCR, per-tool authz, in-process rate-limiting, AWS deployment)'
No occurrence of 'out of scope' appears anywhere in the plan body text
findings.finding: The AC1 oracle greps for the literal string `'out of scope'` (`grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`) to verify the residual-risks section. However, the plan body consistently uses 'deferred' (e.g. 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred', 'explicitly deferred items') — never 'out of scope'. If the author follows the plan's own vocabulary, the AC1 oracle will fail on a correctly-written document.
findings.location: AC1 oracle: `grep -Eq 'out of scope' docs/mcp-auth.md`; What section: 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred'
scenarios: An author faithfully uses 'deferred' language from the plan body in the residual-risks section; the AC1 grep oracle fails, causing a false build failure.
An author force-inserts 'out of scope' just to pass the oracle, disconnecting the wording from the plan's intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the oracle to: `grep -Eq '(out of scope|deferred)' docs/mcp-auth.md`

findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)' is one of the three interactive-client connection paths. 'CIMD' is not a recognized OAuth/OIDC specification acronym — it does not appear in RFC 6749, RFC 7591 (DCR), RFC 9156 (CIBA), or the MCP 2025-06-18 specification — making this an unverified capability assertion that operators trying to follow the guide will find unactionable.
findings.location: What section — docs/mcp-auth.md spec bullet: 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'

findings.finding: The acronym 'CIMD' is used in the client-connection-paths documentation requirement without being expanded or defined anywhere in the plan, the existing docs, or the codebase.
findings.location: What section — '(2) automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact Claude Code CLI flag name for injecting a bearer header (run `claude --help` or check the official Claude Code docs) and the exact Claude.ai MCP client config key for static headers, and update the plan/guide text to use the confirmed names.
evidence: Plan text: '(1) static bearer via Claude Code `--header "Authorization: Bearer …"` / Claude.ai `static_headers`'
search_files for 'Claude Code --header' → no matches in repo
search_files for 'static_headers' → no matches in repo (only in cached plan-review artifacts)
Evidence class: Unverified — no in-repo --help output or docs citation; relies on training-time memory of external product CLI
findings.finding: The operator guide spec instructs end-users to connect via Claude Code `--header "Authorization: Bearer …"` and Claude.ai `static_headers` as named flag/config-key surfaces on external products. Neither flag name appears anywhere in the repo, and neither has been verified against Claude Code --help output or official Claude.ai MCP client documentation. If the actual flag is `--add-header`, `--headers`, or `--bearer`, or if `static_headers` is a different key name, the operator guide ships with broken copy-paste commands.
findings.location: What section — 'The three interactive-client connection paths'; AC1 oracle (grep 'interactive-client')
scenarios: Operator follows the guide and passes `--header` to Claude Code but the real flag is `--add-header` or similar — connection silently fails with no useful error.
Claude.ai desktop app uses a different config key name than `static_headers` — operator's config is ignored and auth fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` (Operator section) to the 'Scope' and 'What' sections as an explicit edit target, since AC2's docs-index gate mandates linking `mcp-auth.md` from it.
evidence: Scope: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only. No code behavior.'
AC2: 'docs/mcp-auth.md is linked from docs/README.md (under the operator/reference section) so the docs-index completeness gate passes'
scripts/check_docs_index.py confirmed live in .github/workflows/test.yml:276 and gerrit-verify.yaml:235
docs/README.md confirmed to exist; its Operator section at lines 77-101 is where mcp-auth.md must be inserted
findings.finding: docs/README.md is an unenumerated edit target: AC2 requires mcp-auth.md to be linked from docs/README.md (enforced by the live check_docs_index.py CI gate), but docs/README.md appears in neither the 'Scope' section ('docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only') nor the 'What' section's deliverable list.
findings.location: Scope section; What section; AC2
scenarios: An executor reading only 'Scope' concludes the sole file edits are mcp-auth.md and the new ADR, skips updating docs/README.md, and the CI check_docs_index.py gate fails.
A code-review tooling system scanning declared edit targets misses docs/README.md and flags an undeclared change.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, change to: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, docs/README.md (Operator section link), the ADR under docs/adr/, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only.' In the What section, add a bullet: 'Update docs/README.md — add a `mcp-auth.md` entry under the Operator section so the docs-index completeness gate (check_docs_index.py) passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a bullet to the What section explicitly stating that `docs/README.md` must be updated to link `docs/mcp-auth.md` under the operator/reference section, matching AC2's requirement.
evidence: What section exhaustively describes mcp-auth.md content and the ADR and reference regeneration, but never states 'update docs/README.md to link the new file'
AC2: 'docs/mcp-auth.md is linked from docs/README.md (under the operator/reference section)'
findings.finding: The deliverable 'link docs/mcp-auth.md from docs/README.md' appears only in AC2 but is absent from the What section, leaving it as an orphan criterion with no corresponding described work.
findings.location: What section (omission) vs. AC2
scenarios: An implementer reading only the What section will not know they must also edit docs/README.md; the CI gate in AC2 may then catch a surprise failure at review time rather than during implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Under the What section, add: '- Update `docs/README.md`: add a link to `docs/mcp-auth.md` under the operator/reference section so the `check_docs_index.py` drift gate passes.'

findings.finding: Creating `docs/mcp-auth.md` without also updating `docs/README.md` to link it will cause the live docs-index completeness gate (scripts/check_docs_index.py, wired into test.yml:276 and gerrit-verify.yaml:235) to fail with 'unindexed living doc', blocking the `make test` pass required by AC4.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (`make lint && make test` pass); also the 'What' section describing `docs/mcp-auth.md`
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, append: 'Update `docs/README.md`'s Operator section to add an entry for `mcp-auth.md` (the docs-index completeness gate in CI will fail the build if the file is not linked from the index).' In AC1, append: '… and `docs/README.md` links the new file under the Operator section so the docs-index gate passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC step (with observable oracle) confirming the new ADR is registered in the ADR index (e.g., `grep -q 'mcp-auth' docs/adr/README.md`) or extend check_docs_index.py to cover docs/adr/ entries so the ADR is discoverable and drift-gated.
evidence: AC3 states only 'An ADR file exists under docs/adr/' with no oracle verifying it is indexed or linked
AC2's oracle is 'python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check' scoped to the new docs/mcp-auth.md file; no equivalent gate is specified for the ADR
Absence: no mention of updating docs/adr/README.md or any ADR index file
findings.finding: The plan specifies an ADR file under docs/adr/ but provides no oracle or AC step confirming the ADR is added to the ADR index (e.g., docs/adr/README.md or equivalent listing), and check_docs_index.py is only wired to docs/README.md for docs/mcp-auth.md. If the project maintains an ADR index, the new ADR will be an undiscoverable orphan unless the index is explicitly updated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('An ADR file exists under docs/adr/…') and AC2 (check_docs_index.py oracle covers only docs/mcp-auth.md)
scenarios: A contributor or operator searching for architecture rationale in docs/adr/ finds no index entry for the new ADR and may miss it
If check_docs_index.py does not scan docs/adr/, the ADR index can drift without CI catching it
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'observable oracle: grep -q "mcp-auth" docs/adr/README.md (or equivalent index file) exits 0' — and update docs/adr/README.md as part of S7's deliverables.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required section (or subsection) to `docs/mcp-auth.md` spec and a corresponding AC grep check covering operator-visible failure states: 401/WWW-Authenticate response shape on auth failure, misconfiguration error messages (e.g., invalid `auth_resource_server_url`), and the fail-closed vs fail-open behavior when the upstream IdP/introspection endpoint is unreachable.
evidence: The 'What' enumeration lists enabling, verifier modes, security model, behind-proxy, three connection paths, and residual risks — but no 'error states / failure responses' entry

The AC grep oracle checks for '^## 1\\. Enabling', section headings for each verifier mode, 'single audience', 'Behind a proxy', 'interactive-client', 'out of scope' — zero checks for any error-state or misconfiguration-diagnostic section

The three interactive-client connection paths are listed only as happy-path flows (static bearer, auto-OAuth, pre-registered client); what operators/clients observe on failure is absent from both spec and AC
findings.finding: The operator guide spec (`docs/mcp-auth.md`) and its AC oracle only verify happy-path section headings; no requirement exists to document operator-visible error/failure states (e.g., 401 response shape, WWW-Authenticate header content on auth failure, misconfiguration diagnostics, client-visible error when `auth_resource_server_url` is wrong, IdP-unreachable behavior, token-expired vs wrong-audience distinction). An operator following the guide will have no documented mental model of what failures look like.
findings.location: ## What — `docs/mcp-auth.md` bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet and its grep oracle
scenarios: Operator misconfigures `auth_resource_server_url` as HTTP for non-loopback — docs don't say what error is returned or where to look
Client presents expired token — operator has no documented expectation of the 401 body or WWW-Authenticate fields to cross-reference
IdP introspection endpoint is unreachable — operator doesn't know whether the server fails open, fails closed, or returns a specific error
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' bullet for `docs/mcp-auth.md`, add: 'A **Troubleshooting / failure-mode reference**: 401 response body and `WWW-Authenticate` header on each failure class (bad token, expired, wrong audience, missing); `auth_resource_server_url` validation error on startup; introspection/proxy verifier behavior when the upstream IdP is unreachable (fail-closed, logged at ERROR).' Then add a grep to the AC oracle: `grep -Eq 'Troubleshooting' docs/mcp-auth.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S1–S6 deliver `required_scopes` as a config key before S7 documents it as an existing gate, and add an explicit S6→S7 dependency assertion naming `required_scopes` as a pre-condition for the operator guide's residual-risks section.
evidence: search_files(src/, 'required_scopes') → no matches anywhere in src/
Plan phrase: 'gated only by server-level `required_scopes` and the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates'
REBAR_MCP_READONLY, ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC, ALLOW_LLM all verified present in src/; required_scopes is not
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'server-level `required_scopes`' as an existing gate alongside `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_LLM`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` in the Residual Risks section of the operator guide. A grep of `src/` returns zero matches for `required_scopes`. It is an S1–S6 deliverable, not a currently-existing mechanism. If S7 documents it as an existing gate before S1–S6 land it, the operator guide will contain a false claim; if it is absent from S1–S6, S7 cannot document it accurately.
findings.location: What section / 'Residual risks' bullet: 'gated only by server-level `required_scopes` and the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates'
scenarios: S7 ships with operator guide stating `required_scopes` is a live gate; an operator enables it and finds no such config key exists.
S1–S6 are incomplete or renamed the key; S7 documents a key that never lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the ADR plan whether 'standalone fastmcp' refers to the PyPI `fastmcp` package (distinct from the Anthropic `mcp` SDK) or the official SDK's auth surface, and substantiate the 'semver-exempt auth / CVE history' claim with a citable reference (changelog entry, package version policy, or published advisory) before committing that rationale.
evidence: Plan text: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone `fastmcp` (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)'
resolve_symbol confirms mcp.server.fastmcp.FastMCP EXISTS at site-packages/mcp/server/fastmcp/__init__.py — this is the official `mcp` SDK, not a standalone package
src/rebar/mcp_server.py:243 already imports `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP`
No CVE or semver-exemption evidence for either the official SDK or the standalone fastmcp PyPI package is cited or searchable in the repo
findings.finding: The plan asserts the ADR will justify the build-own verifiers decision over 'standalone `fastmcp` (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)' — but this is an unverified capability claim. The installed `fastmcp` in this project is `mcp.server.fastmcp` from the official Anthropic `mcp` SDK package (confirmed resolvable), not a standalone third-party package. The plan's characterization of 'semver-exempt auth' and 'CVE history' for fastmcp is either (a) conflating the official SDK with the standalone `fastmcp` PyPI package, or (b) an ungrounded assertion about the official SDK's auth stability — neither is substantiated in the plan or verifiable from the codebase.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone `fastmcp` (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)'
scenarios: An ADR reviewer asks for the CVE history that motivated rejecting standalone fastmcp and finds no citable evidence — the build-own justification is ungrounded.
If 'standalone fastmcp' refers to a specific third-party PyPI package distinct from the mcp SDK, the ADR conflates the two and the rejection rationale applies to the wrong artifact.
If the official SDK's auth surface IS semver-exempt, the ADR should cite the SDK's own changelog/versioning policy to substantiate this claim.
findings.suggested_fix: If the concern is the standalone `fastmcp` PyPI package (by @jlowin/fastmcp), cite its versioning policy and any published advisories explicitly. If the concern is the official `mcp` SDK's auth stability, cite the SDK's changelog or issue tracker showing breaking auth changes across minor versions. Either way, separate the two packages clearly in the ADR so the rejection rationale is traceable.

findings.finding: AC1's proving command for content coverage is vague — 'a docs link/section check passes' is not a concrete, executable command; an executor cannot confirm which script/grep to run to verify all required subsections are present.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('observable oracle: the file exists and a docs link/section check passes')
findings.suggested_fix: Example oracle addition: `grep -qE '^## (Security model|Behind-a-proxy|Client connection paths|Residual risks)' docs/mcp-auth.md` — or reference a named script (`scripts/check_doc_sections.py docs/mcp-auth.md`) that exits non-zero if any required heading is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `make typecheck` to S7 AC4 so it reads `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass, fulfilling the parent epic AC8 requirement that all three gates pass on the final integrated branch.
evidence: S7 AC4: '`make lint && make test` pass (docs/mechanical leaf; no typecheck-relevant code added here)'
Parent epic AC8: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the final integrated branch.'
S7 Why: 'S7 confirms the integrated whole is consistent' — S7 is the designated integration/verification story.
S7 Scope: 'Final reference/manifest consistency check … S7 verifies the union' — positions S7 as the integration gate holder.
S1–S6 each run typecheck per-story, but none are scoped to verify the *combined* branch that S7 is meant to certify.
findings.finding: S7 AC4 omits `make typecheck`, stating 'docs/mechanical leaf; no typecheck-relevant code added here', but the parent epic AC8 requires `make lint && make typecheck && make test` to pass 'on the final integrated branch.' S7 is explicitly the terminal story responsible for confirming the integrated S1–S6+S7 branch is consistent; no sibling story is positioned to verify the combined-branch typecheck state. This redefines the parent's integration gate more narrowly than the parent permits.
findings.location: S7 Acceptance Criteria, AC4 vs parent epic (3393-83ca-5501-4e6e) Acceptance Criteria AC8
scenarios: A type error introduced by a sibling story's code change (S1–S6) slips through on the combined branch because S7 — the only story verifying integrated state — does not require typecheck to pass.
The epic closes with all child stories green, but `make typecheck` silently fails on the final integrated branch, violating parent AC8.
findings.suggested_fix: Change S7 AC4 to: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the integrated branch — S7 is the terminal story and must assert the full gate set specified by parent AC8, even though S7 adds no new typecheckable code (a clean pass is still required to confirm S1–S6 left no type errors).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/golden/config_surface.json` regeneration to AC4's final consistency check, alongside `mcp-reference.md`, `env-vars.md`, and `server.json`, since new S1–S6 MCP auth config keys require updating that golden to keep `test_config_surface_golden.py` green.
evidence: tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py exists and pins config_keys + canonical_env_vars
tests/golden/config_surface.json already enumerates REBAR_MCP_READONLY, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM
AC4 text names only mcp-reference.md, env-vars.md, and server.json — config_surface.json is absent
New S1–S6 auth keys added to _SECTIONS will require config_surface.json regeneration to pass test_config_surface_golden.py
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 final consistency check names three drift gates (`docs/mcp-reference.md`, `docs/env-vars.md`, `server.json` via `check_server_manifest.py`) but omits `tests/golden/config_surface.json`, which is a verified, live drift-tracking golden that pins every config key + canonical env var and must be regenerated when new S1–S6 MCP auth keys are added to `_SECTIONS`. The test (`test_config_surface_golden.py`) fails the build if a key is added without updating the golden.
findings.location: AC4: 'docs/mcp-reference.md and docs/env-vars.md are fully regenerated … and the CI drift gates for them and for server.json pass'
scenarios: S7's final consistency check passes its named three gates but CI fails on test_config_surface_golden.py because the golden was not updated for new S1–S6 auth config keys.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'and `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is regenerated (run `python scripts/...` or update manually) so `tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py` passes on the combined branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add regeneration of `tests/golden/config_surface.json` (via `python scripts/gen_config_surface_golden.py` or equivalent) to the AC4 consistency-check scope, so the new S1–S6 MCP auth config keys are captured in the anti-regression guard.
evidence: AC4 states: 'docs/mcp-reference.md and docs/env-vars.md are fully regenerated for the integrated new-key set and the CI drift gates for them and for server.json pass'
tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py exists and guards tests/golden/config_surface.json — a fourth derived artifact not mentioned in AC4
tests/golden/config_surface.json currently has no mcp.auth_* keys (listing ends at mcp.readonly, mcp.allow_jira_sync, mcp.allow_llm)
The Approach section says 'regeneration (not hand-editing) keeps the derived docs authoritative' but does not list config_surface.json as a regeneration target
findings.finding: The 'final reference/manifest consistency check' scope (AC4) omits `tests/golden/config_surface.json` from the list of artifacts to regenerate. S1–S6 add new `mcp.*` config keys (e.g. auth_resource_server_url, auth_strategies entries); the golden file must be explicitly regenerated to capture these additions. While `test_config_surface_golden.py` won't cause `make test` to fail for *added* (not removed) keys, the plan's claim that S7 'confirms the integrated whole is consistent' is undermined if the config-surface golden is left stale — a future key removal will fail against a golden that never captured the S1–S6 additions, producing a confusing false-negative.
findings.location: AC4 / 'Final reference/manifest consistency check' section
scenarios: S7 implementer runs 'make test' per AC4/AC5, it passes (new keys don't trigger the golden test), but config_surface.json is never updated — the new auth config surface is invisible to the anti-regression guard.
A future story removes an auth key legitimately but the golden was never updated to include it, so the tombstone requirement is never triggered — silent regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require `docs/mcp-auth.md` to include, co-located with every flat-config example that contains a credential field (static token, JWT secret, introspection client_secret, proxy header), an explicit instruction that the secret VALUE must be supplied via environment-variable substitution or a gitignored `.env` and must NEVER be committed in plaintext — referencing or quoting the 'Secrets live only in the environment' rule from `docs/config.md` — and add a corresponding grep assertion to the AC1 oracle (e.g. `grep -Eq 'never.*commit|env.*variable|gitignore' docs/mcp-auth.md`).
evidence: Plan (What): 'each of the five verifier modes (static, jwt, introspection, proxy, custom) with a flat-config example each'
Plan (What / security model): 'tokens/secrets never logged' — covers logging only, silent on committed-config placement
Plan (What / residual risks): 'mandated ≥128-bit static tokens (document the generator one-liner)' — documents entropy requirement but not where the value lives

docs/config.md line 34-36: 'Secrets live only in the environment / a gitignored .env, never the committed config' — this rule exists but the plan does not require mcp-auth.md to cross-reference or restate it for auth credentials
AC1 oracle: grep checks for section headings and 'out of scope' text; no check for 'env var' / 'do not commit' / 'secrets manager' co-located with examples
OWASP A07:2021 (Identification and Authentication Failures) / T5c secret-lifecycle dimension
findings.finding: The flat-config examples for verifier modes (especially `static` bearer token, and potentially JWT/introspection shared-secret keys) will contain literal credential placeholders, but the plan does not require docs/mcp-auth.md to instruct operators that these secret values must NOT be placed as plaintext in committed config and must instead use env-var injection or a gitignored .env — leaving operators who follow only the auth guide at risk of committing bearer tokens or HMAC secrets to git.
findings.location: What section — docs/mcp-auth.md spec ('a flat-config example each'); AC1 grep oracle (no 'do not commit' / 'env var' assertion required); security model bullet ('tokens/secrets never logged' covers logging but not storage placement)
scenarios: An operator reads only docs/mcp-auth.md, copies the static-verifier flat-config example literally into their committed rebar.toml, and pushes a live bearer token to a public or semi-public git repo.
An operator configures a JWT verifier with an HMAC shared secret in committed config; the secret is subsequently exposed via a git history leak or repo fork.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's mcp-auth.md spec, add a bullet: 'Each flat-config example that contains a credential field (static token value, JWT secret, client_secret, proxy header value) MUST include an inline callout: "Never commit this value in plaintext — supply it via the REBAR_<KEY> environment variable or a gitignored .env (see docs/config.md §Settings vs. secrets)."' Also extend the AC1 grep oracle with: `grep -Eq 'never commit|env.*var|gitignore' docs/mcp-auth.md`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
